claim_text: Grouped in 'AI Scientist systems have proliferated rapidly'.
evidence: Manuscript L69 (BG): grouped '...have proliferated rapidly~\citep{boiko2023emergent,...}'. Coscientist is a canonical member of the autonomous-agent-for-science wave and the paper explicitly situates itself among such systems: 'This work has been done independently and in parallel to other works on autonomous agents23-25, with ChemCrow26 serving as another example in the chemistry domain' (l.57-59), listing Auto-GPT, BabyAGI and LangChain (refs 23-25). Published Nature, Dec 2023, at the front of the proliferation the manuscript describes. Grouped background citation is fully supportable.
fix: None. Keep as-is.

claim_text: Characterized as a tool-augmented synthesis agent (Coscientist).
evidence: Manuscript L69 (CHAR): 'tool-augmented synthesis agents (Coscientist~\citep{boiko2023emergent})'. 'Tool-augmented' is exact: the architecture IS a set of tool commands -- 'System prompts... are engineered in a modular fashion, described as four commands that define the action space: GOOGLE, PYTHON, DOCUMENTATION and EXPERIMENT' (l.75-77), with internet/documentation search, Docker code execution and robotic experimentation APIs (abstract l.19-21). 'Synthesis agent' is supported: Coscientist plans syntheses of known compounds (Fig 2) and actually designs+performs synthesis -- Suzuki-Miyaura and Sonogashira cross-couplings executed on an OT-2 with products confirmed by GC-MS (l.723-761). Minor (non-defect): Coscientist is broader than synthesis alone (it also does reaction-condition optimization and hardware control), so 'synthesis agent' narrows its scope slightly, but this is a fair label, not an overclaim.
fix: None required. (Optional, LOW: 'tool-augmented research/synthesis agent' would capture that Coscientist also does optimization/hardware control, but the current phrasing is accurate.)

claim_text: Prior work gives qualitative characterization of individual systems, no shared framework.
evidence: Manuscript L90 (BG): 'Prior work~\citep{...,boiko2023emergent} provides qualitative characterizations ... no shared framework'. Boiko characterizes Coscientist qualitatively -- 'Our findings demonstrate the versatility, efficacy and explainability of artificial intelligence systems like Coscientist' (abstract l.24-26) -- and presents a proof of concept (l.1699) without proposing any shared/standardized framework for comparing autonomy or capability across systems. (The paper does define one internal quantitative metric, 'normalized advantage', for its optimization game, l.1642-1657, but that is task-internal, not a cross-system evaluative framework, so it does not contradict the 'no shared framework' grouping.) Supportable as a grouped meta-characterization.
fix: None. Keep as-is.

claim_text: Coscientist has only constrained task-level feedback; measured outcomes inform later choices; loop is partial; novelty/significance validation human-mediated.
evidence: Manuscript L166 (CHAR): 'Coscientist has only constrained task-level feedback (measured outcomes inform later choices~\citep{boiko2023emergent}); its loop is \emph{partial}, with novelty/significance validation human-mediated.' PART A ('measured outcomes inform later choices' / 'constrained... partial'): DEFENSIBLE and the hedges are apt. The only loop where outcomes inform later choices is the optimization 'game' (Fig 6): Coscientist selects conditions, is told the yield, lists observations, then chooses again; 'The normalized advantage values increase over time, suggesting that the model can effectively reuse the information obtained to provide more specific guidance on reactivity' (l.1674-1676). BUT precision nuance: those yields are LOOKUPS from pre-collected published datasets, not fresh measurements -- 'any reaction proposed by Coscientist would be within these datasets and accessible as a lookup table' (l.1628-1629), over Perera et al. (Suzuki, flow, l.1623-1624) and Doyle's Buchwald-Hartwig set (l.1625-1627). The one genuine physical measurement -- GC-MS of the real Suzuki/Sonogashira products (l.753-761) -- was interpreted by the authors post hoc and NOT fed back into a next Coscientist decision (single design->execute). So 'measured outcomes' is true only in the benchmark-lookup sense; the physical-measurement->decision loop is OPEN. The software error-correction loop IS closed ('the language model behind the Planner enables code to be fixed in case of software errors', l.194; wrong heater-shaker method -> consults docs -> corrects, l.749-752), which supports 'partial'. PART B ('novelty/significance validation human-mediated'): OVERSTATES slightly. Coscientist performs NO novelty/significance validation -- all synthesis targets are known compounds (aspirin, acetaminophen, benzoic acid, ethyl acetate, nitroaniline, ibuprofen, phenolphthalein, Fig 2a), reactions are textbook named reactions (Suzuki-Miyaura, Sonogashira), and optimization runs over known datasets; the paper makes no discovery/novelty claim (self-described '(semi-)autonomous... proof of concept', l.1699-1701). 'Human-mediated' implies a novelty/significance-validation step exists but is done by humans; more precisely it is ABSENT/out-of-scope. Net: the manuscript's core thesis (partial loop; no autonomous novelty/significance validation) is faithful; both points are refinements.
fix: KEEP the claim (partial-loop characterization is correct and well-hedged). Two OPTIONAL precision improvements (LOW/MED): (1) For 'measured outcomes inform later choices', note that in Boiko the closed feedback is over pre-collected benchmark yields used as a lookup table (Perera/Doyle datasets), not fresh instrument measurements -- the one physical measurement (GC-MS) is analysed by humans and not fed back; e.g. '...measured (benchmark-lookup) outcomes inform later choices'. (2) For 'novelty/significance validation human-mediated', consider 'absent/out-of-scope' rather than 'human-mediated', since Coscientist neither performs nor reports any novelty/significance validation (all targets are known compounds/reactions/datasets). Neither change is required for the sentence to be defensible.

claim_text: Uses a 1-10 novelty/significance score collapsing distinct properties into one scalar.
evidence: Factual anchor 'The AI Scientist's 1--10 score' is supported: idea-generation ratings are explicitly 'A rating from 1 to 10' for Interestingness, Feasibility, and Novelty (App A.1 prompt, fulltext L1775, L1781-1782); the reviewer's Overall recommendation uses the NeurIPS 1-10 scale (score 6 = 'Weak Accept', ~avg accepted paper; L361-362, case-study 'Overall: 5' L675). HOWEVER the characterization 'collapse [novelty/significance/correctness] into one scalar' is a mild oversimplification: the reviewer actually outputs SEPARATE sub-dimensions (Soundness, Presentation, Contribution, Originality, Quality, Clarity, Significance, Overall, Confidence; case-study review JSON L646-676) rather than literally reducing everything to one number — though the accept/reject DECISION is thresholded on the single Overall scalar, which fits the manuscript's point. Core claim accurate; the 'collapse into one scalar' framing is defensible as decision-level but not literal.
fix: Optional tightening: '...collapse these into one decision scalar (e.g., The AI Scientist thresholds an accept/reject on a single 1--10 overall score, despite emitting separate soundness/significance/etc. sub-scores).' Not required — current wording is within acceptable citation tolerance.

claim_text: Gives the most V layers in the class; internal automated acceptance gate stays uncalibrated.
evidence: Manuscript's characterization ('the biased V_s reviewer stays uncalibrated') is an editorial inference that is GROUNDED in the paper's own data, though the paper never uses the words 'biased' or 'uncalibrated' for the reviewer (the token 'bias' appears in the paper ONLY as 'colour-biased MNIST dataset', an experiment example, p.917-fig). Support for accept-bias/miscalibration: Table 1 (p.915) Automated Reviewer FPR = 0.45±0.10 (pre-cutoff) and 0.52±0.10 (post-cutoff) versus Human FPR = 0.17 — i.e., the automated reviewer wrongly accepts ~half of true-reject papers, far more lenient than humans. Contamination self-admission: 'data contamination may exist, as balanced decision accuracy decreases from 69% before to 66% in the year after the cutoff' (p.916). Both substantiate 'biased/uncalibrated' as a fair, data-backed critique. It is the manuscript's own analytical judgment, NOT a false claim attributed to the paper.
fix: Optional strengthening: cite Table 1 FPR (0.45-0.52 vs human 0.17) as the concrete basis for 'biased/uncalibrated', since the paper does not state those words narratively. Not required for accuracy.

claim_text: Grouped in 'proliferated rapidly'.
evidence: Manuscript L69 groups schmidgall2025agentlab among 'AI Scientist systems---agents that independently generate hypotheses, execute experiments, and synthesize knowledge---[that] have proliferated rapidly'. Category-level membership is VALID: Agent Laboratory is an autonomous LLM research framework and legitimately part of this proliferation (paper abstract p.1: 'autonomous LLM-based framework capable of completing the entire research process'; Discussion line 845: 'builds upon an emerging trend in the use of language agents for science'). HOWEVER the umbrella gloss 'independently generate hypotheses' does NOT fit this specific member: Agent Laboratory explicitly 'takes as input a human research idea' (lines 15, 31, 63) and is 'designed to assist human scientists in executing their own research ideas' (lines 62-63), contrasted against systems 'where agents participate in their own research ideation independent of human input' (lines 61-62) and 'fully automated research pipelines that attempt to conceive their own research directions' (lines 824-826). Even in autonomous mode the research question is human-provided (5 templates, lines 428-441), not agent-generated. Acceptable as a grouped background citation about proliferation, but the descriptor mismatch is recorded as a reverse finding.
fix: No hard change required for a grouped proliferation cite. If precision desired, the category gloss 'independently generate hypotheses' should be softened, since Agent Laboratory (a human-idea co-pilot) does not generate its own hypotheses; consider noting the human-in-the-loop members of the group are idea-driven rather than idea-generating.

claim_text: Prior qualitative characterization, no shared framework.

Minor imprecision: 'qualitative characterizations' understates the paper, which also provides substantial QUANTITATIVE evaluation (human + automated NeurIPS-style reviewer scores, per-phase cost/time, success rates, MLE-Bench medal counts). But the core claim ('no shared framework for cross-system comparison') is true, so the grouped BG cite is accurate.

claim_text: Agent Laboratory documents +2.3-point systematic over-estimation relative to human PhD students (NOTE: sentence cites kim2026aireviewers, not schmidgall) -- check attribution.
evidence: Manuscript L166 (full sentence): 'Agent Laboratory delegates $V_s$ (NeurIPS-guideline LLM reviewer) but documents $+$2.3-point systematic over-estimation relative to human PhD students---adding a $V$ layer does not guarantee completeness if the layer is unreliable~\citep{kim2026aireviewers}.' The +2.3 CONTENT is faithfully Agent Laboratory's OWN documented finding, so the fact itself is ACCURATE: (1) 'delegates V_s (NeurIPS-guideline LLM reviewer)' -> paper-solver Paper Review uses an LLM reviewer simulating NeurIPS review, adapted from Lu et al. 2024b (lines 348-354); (2) the +2.3 over-estimation is documented verbatim -- 'While the automated reviewers gave an average overall above average NeurIPS paper score of 6.1/10, human reviewers provided a much lower average of 3.8/10 (-2.3 points)' (lines 511-513; also Intro contribution 3, lines 76-81: 'automated scores significantly overestimating quality (6.1/10 vs. 3.8/10 overall)'; Fig 6 caption, lines 519-524: 'an average of -2.3 points lower'). Scale is NeurIPS-style overall 1-10; the human comparators were 10 volunteer PhD students who reviewed the 15 AI-generated papers (lines 442-443). The manuscript's '+2.3' over-estimation sign convention (automated over human) is correct and consistent with the paper's '-2.3' (human minus automated). PROBLEM: this Agent-Laboratory-origin fact is attributed to \citep{kim2026aireviewers} instead of \citep{schmidgall2025agentlab}. Cross-confirmed independently (findings.md HIGH): kim2026aireviewers contains zero '+2.3' occurrences and never mentions Agent Laboratory, while schmidgall2025agentlab documents it as a primary result.
fix: MISATTRIBUTION -- change/add citation for the +2.3 fact from \citep{kim2026aireviewers} to \citep{schmidgall2025agentlab} (Agent Laboratory is the primary source that documents this finding; keep kim2026aireviewers only if it is a legitimate secondary/aggregating source, but schmidgall2025agentlab must be the primary attribution). SECONDARY (IMPRECISE): 'over-estimation relative to human PhD students' should read 'relative to human PhD-student REVIEWERS of the AI-generated papers' -- the comparison is the LLM reviewer over-scoring the SAME AI-generated papers relative to what the human PhD-student reviewers assigned (automated 6.1/10 vs human 3.8/10), NOT the system's papers being rated +2.3 above PhD students' own work.

claim_text: Grouped in 'proliferated rapidly'.

'AI Scientist system' is a loose umbrella label (that specific term is not used by the paper), but for a grouped 'proliferated rapidly' background claim the citation is legitimate and not load-bearing on specifics.

claim_text: Characterized as a formal-mathematics agent (Aletheia).
evidence: L69 labels Aletheia a 'formal-mathematics agent (Aletheia~\citep{google2026aletheia})'. This inverts the paper. p.3 l.154-155 verbatim: 'In contrast to AlphaGeometry (Chervonyi et al., 2025; Trinh et al., 2024) and AlphaProof (Hubert et al., 2025), that use formal language, Aletheia operates end-to-end in natural language.' Abstract l.16-17 and l.41 likewise state Aletheia works 'end-to-end in natural language'. Aletheia is precisely the NON-formal system; the formal-mathematics agents are AlphaProof/AlphaGeometry. The manuscript is also internally inconsistent: C023 (L176) labels this same cite 'NL' and C024 (L438) calls it 'natural-language proofs'.

If a genuine formal-mathematics-agent exemplar is needed at that spot, cite AlphaProof (Hubert et al., 2025) or AlphaGeometry (Trinh et al., 2024) instead, and keep Aletheia only on the natural-language side.

claim_text: Listed under mathematics, natural-language.

claim_text: Produces natural-language proofs scored by LLM and human judgment; loop closes mechanically but V_sem imperfect.
evidence: L438: 'Aletheia~\citep{google2026aletheia} produces natural-language proofs scored by LLM and human judgment. The loop closes mechanically, but V_sem is imperfect...'. All four sub-claims supported: (1) 'natural-language proofs' -> p.3 l.155, abstract l.16-17. (2) 'scored by LLM and human judgment' -> 'Aletheia's informal verifier mechanism streamlined the initial pool ... the model returned 212 responses as potentially correct. These candidates were then evaluated by a team of mathematicians' (l.380-382); 'verified semi-autonomously using both AI grading and human experts' (l.62-63). (3) 'loop closes mechanically' -> Generator/Verifier/Reviser subagents 'interact continuously until a solution is found that the Verifier approves, or until the attempts reach a preset limit' (p.3, Fig.1). (4) 'V_sem imperfect' -> 'Even with its verifier mechanism, Aletheia is still more prone to errors than human experts' (l.863); 'Natural language verification by human experts inherently involves some subjectivity' (fn.15, l.711).

claim_text: Of 200 audited Erdos solutions only 13 (6.5%) rated meaningfully correct.
evidence: L438 number: 'of 200 audited Erdos solutions only 13 (6.5%) were rated meaningfully correct.' Exact match to paper: 'out of the 200 solution candidates that we were definitely able to mark correct or incorrect, 137 (68.5%) of the responses were fundamentally flawed, while 63 (31.5%) of the responses were technically correct, of which only 13 (6.5%) were meaningfully correct' (p.9, l.402-405). Table 6 confirms: Meaningfully Correct 13 = 6.5%, Total Candidates 200, title 'Solution accuracy on 200 AI-generated responses, as graded by humans' (l.755). Denominator (200 human-graded candidates), count (13), percentage (6.5%), and the stricter label 'meaningfully correct' (vs the looser 31.5%/'technically correct') are all faithful.

claim_text: Grouped in 'proliferated rapidly'.
evidence: L69 (BG) groups liu2026lasthuman in a multi-cite '...proliferated rapidly~\citep{...,liu2026lasthuman}'. liu2026lasthuman (arXiv:2604.24658v2, 29 Apr 2026) is a bona fide recent agent-native-research-artifact protocol and positions itself amid 'A growing line of work argues that scientific knowledge should be authored in machine-readable form' (Sec.8, Related Work). As one member of a grouped background citation about the rapid emergence of agent-native research artifacts/tooling, its inclusion is defensible. (Note: the subject noun of 'proliferated rapidly' is not in the supplied verbatim snippet, so adjudication is on membership-in-class, which holds.)

claim_text: Addresses the artifact layer -- output format and verification protocol -- rather than the discovery loop.
evidence: Fair characterization. liu2026lasthuman's core contribution IS the artifact layer: a four-layer output format (Sec.2.2) plus a verification protocol (the ARA Seal, Sec.5.2, 'verification protocol (Figure 8)', line ~580). It explicitly distinguishes itself from the discovery loop, contrasting with 'autonomous research agents that conduct experiments end-to-end (Boiko et al., 2023; M. Bran et al., 2024; Schmidgall et al., 2025; Baek et al., 2025)' (Sec.8, lines 1176-1180) and stating ARA 'encodes claims, evidence, heuristics, and their executable bindings at authoring time' (line 1189). The paper does capture the research PROCESS via the Live Research Manager/Exploration Graph, but as artifact structure, not as an autonomous discovery agent -- so 'address the artifact layer ... rather than the discovery loop itself' is accurate.

claim_text: ARA replaces narrative paper with a four-layer ontology (/logic,/src,/trace,/evidence); Seal automates V_syntax, V_semantic (Rigor Auditor), V_empirical; reserves V_human for significance/novelty.
evidence: All components verified. Four-layer ontology /logic, /src, /trace, /evidence: confirmed Sec.2.2 (Cognitive/Physical/Exploration Graph/Evidence Layers). ARA Seal three automated levels map cleanly to the manuscript's V_syntax/V_semantic/V_empirical: Level 1 Structural Integrity (schema/structural = V_syntax); Level 2 Argumentative Rigor = the Rigor Auditor (= V_semantic); Level 3 Execution Reproducibility, run by a 'sandboxed coding agent' (Fig.8) = V_empirical. 'reserving V_human for significance and novelty' matches Stage 3 human review, which focuses on 'significance, novelty, and taste' (Sec.5.1 P1; Sec.5.3 Stage 3). '82.6% detection' for the Rigor Auditor matches Table 4 overall (95/115 = 82.6%).

claim_text: Rigor Auditor 82.6% detection.
evidence: Table 4 ('Rigor Auditor effectiveness on the mutation benchmark, 23 ARAs x 5 injection types'), Overall row: n=115, Detected 95 (82.6%). The 82.6% figure is exact. See also Sec.7.5 'catches 100% of three high-severity classes ... and 91% of missing falsifications, but only 22% of orphan experiments'. (Reverse note logged: manuscript's 'detection' vs paper's 'Overall' pooled 95/115 -- both round to 82.6%.)

claim_text: (uncited clause) only 45.4% of papers fully specify reproduction requirements and 90.2% of extension cost is failed exploration.
evidence: Both numbers are genuinely from liu2026lasthuman and correct in value, but the clause carries NO \citep. (1) 45.4% -- Sec.1/Sec.7.1: 'Across PaperBench's 8,921 expert reproduction requirements, only 45.4% are fully specified in the source PDF' (line 726); Table 8 Overall 8,921 -> 45.4% sufficient; App. A.3.3 O4: 'Across 23 PaperBench papers (8,921 rubric requirements), only 45.4% of reproduction requirements are fully specified in the PDF' (lines 1789-1790). SOURCE UNIT = fraction of REQUIREMENTS, not papers. The manuscript's wording 'only 45.4% of papers fully specify reproduction requirements' slips the denominator to 'papers'. (2) 90.2% -- Sec.7.4/App. E.3/Table 10: on RE-Bench (24,008 runs), '90.2% of dollar cost is spent on failed exploration' / '59.2% of agent tokens (and 90.2% of dollar cost) ... spent on dead-end exploration'. Value correct; 'extension cost' is a slightly loose gloss for 'RE-Bench agent-run dollar cost on failed/dead-end exploration' that the extension experiment builds on.

Also tighten the 45.4% wording from 'papers' to requirements, e.g.: 'only 45.4% of reproduction requirements are fully specified in source PDFs (8,921 requirements across 23 PaperBench papers), and 90.2% of RE-Bench dollar cost goes to failed/dead-end exploration~\citep{liu2026lasthuman}.'

claim_text: Rigor Auditor achieves 82.6% average detection across five structural violation types but only 22% on orphaned-experiment violations.
evidence: Both figures exact. Table 4: Overall 95/115 = 82.6% across the five injection types; Orphan experiment 5/23 = 22%. The manuscript's 'five structural violation types' correctly denotes the 5 INJECTION types (Table 4 caption: '23 ARAs x 5 injection types': fabricated claim, missing falsification, orphan experiment, over-claim, rebutted-branch leak), NOT the 6 Rigor Auditor SCORING dimensions -- so the '5 vs 6' trap is avoided. 'only 22% on orphaned-experiment violations' matches the paper's own headline blind spot ('only 22% of orphan experiments', Sec.7.5; App. H.2.2 Table 14 Total detected 5/23).

claim_text: ARA a plausible entry point for shared memory w.
evidence: Appropriately hedged ('a plausible entry point'). liu2026lasthuman positions ARA as a shared substrate / collective memory: Sec.6 (Human+AI)^2 Network describes a 'shared ARA network' and 'a common substrate through which human and machine researchers alike publish, verify, and build on scientific knowledge' (line 1284); Sec.9 'Long term: cross-disciplinary collective memory' and 'a natural substrate for cross-disciplinary knowledge transfer' (lines 1172, 1183); aggregated lineages 'form a queryable scientific knowledge graph'. claim_text: Cited as a specialized predictor without a generation-and-search loop (excluded from scope).
evidence: Manuscript L73 (CHAR, scope-definition): 'We include systems whose primary goal is automated scientific \emph{discovery} through hypothesis-experiment-validation cycles, and exclude specialized predictors without a generation-and-search loop (e.g., AlphaFold~\citep{jumper2021alphafold}).' AlphaFold is cited as the canonical EXCLUSION -- a specialized predictor lacking a generation-and-search discovery loop -- and the paper substantiates every element of that descriptor. (1) SPECIALIZED PREDICTOR: 'The AlphaFold network directly predicts the 3D coordinates of all heavy atoms for a given protein using the primary amino acid sequence and aligned sequences of homologues as inputs' (p.584-585). It performs ONE prediction task (sequence -> structure); Reporting Summary confirms 'no experimental work is described in this study; the results are the output of a computational method' (p. Reporting Summary). (2) NO HYPOTHESIS-EXPERIMENT-VALIDATION CYCLE / NO GENERATION-AND-SEARCH LOOP: the pipeline is a single feedforward map -- genetic/structure DB search -> Evoformer (48 blocks) -> structure module (8 blocks) -> 3D structure (Fig. 1e). There is no proposal of candidate hypotheses evaluated against an external oracle and iterated. The only iteration is internal 'recycling': 'we reinforce the notion of iterative refinement by repeatedly applying the final loss to outputs and then feeding the outputs recursively into the same modules. The iterative refinement using the whole network (which we term recycling and is related to approaches in computer vision)' (p.585) -- i.e. refinement of one structural hypothesis inside the forward pass, three times (Fig. 4b), NOT a generate-evaluate-select discovery cycle. Even the paper's 'the network searches and rearranges secondary structure elements for many layers' (p.587) is metaphorical internal representation refinement across depth, not an external search over hypotheses/experiments. Self-distillation ('use a trained network to predict the structure of around 350,000 diverse sequences from Uniclust30 ... train the same architecture again from scratch', p.587) is a TRAINING-TIME procedure, not an inference-time discovery loop. Therefore excluding AlphaFold from a corpus of automated-discovery (hypothesis-experiment-validation) systems, on the stated ground that it is a specialized predictor without a generation-and-search loop, is fully faithful to the paper. jumper2021alphafold is exactly the right, uncontroversial exemplar for this exclusion.

claim_text: Grounding: model AI Scientist systems as POMDPs (belief b(s) updated by observations).
evidence: The manuscript models systems as POMDPs where the true state s is inaccessible and the system maintains a belief b(s) updated by observations -- this is exactly Kaelbling et al.'s formulation. Section 3.1 (p.105): 'A POMDP is an MDP in which the agent is unable to observe the current state. Instead, it makes an observation based on the action and resulting state.' Section 3.2 (p.106): belief state b is a probability distribution over S, a sufficient statistic, updated by state estimator SE based on last action, current observation, and previous belief state. Section 3.3 (p.107) belief update: b'(s') = O(s',a,o) * sum_{s in S} T(s,a,s') b(s) / Pr(o|a,b). The manuscript's notation b(s) matches the paper's notation exactly. Attribution ('Following ~cite') is correct: the POMDP-with-belief-state formulation is precisely what this paper presents.

claim_text: POMDP framework is classical.
evidence: Calling the POMDP framework 'classical' and citing Kaelbling, Littman & Cassandra (1998) is appropriate: this is a canonical, widely-cited foundational POMDP reference (Artificial Intelligence journal, 1998), which itself recapitulates the classical operations-research POMDP theory (Smallwood & Sondik 1973, Sondik 1971/1978, Monahan 1982, Lovejoy 1991) and presents an exact solution algorithm. It is a standard/foundational grounding citation for the POMDP framework.

claim_text: BED provides a normative criterion (EIG) for the search policy's information objective.
evidence: ["(a) chaloner1995 IS the classical BED review and DOES supply the normative criterion -- CONFIRMED. Abstract (p.273): 'This paper reviews the literature on Bayesian experimental design. A unified view of this topic is presented, based on a decision-theoretic approach.' The normative rule is stated at p.275: Eq. (1) U(eta) = int int U(d,theta,eta,y) p(theta|y,eta) p(y|eta) d theta dy is the expected utility of the best decision for design eta; Eq. (2): 'The Bayesian solution to the experimental design problem is provided by the design eta* maximizing (1)'; and in words: 'a good way to design experiments is to specify a utility function reflecting the purpose of the experiment, to regard the design choice as a decision problem and to select a design that maximizes the expected utility.' => 'provides a normative criterion ... select d* maximizing [expected utility]'.", "(b) The EIG formula in the manuscript is exactly chaloner's information utility. p.277 (Section 2.2): 'several authors (Stone, 1959a,b; DeGroot, 1962, 1986; Bernardo, 1979) considered the expected gain in Shannon information given by an experiment as a utility function (Shannon, 1948). These authors proposed choosing a design that maximizes the expected gain in Shannon information or, equivalently, maximizes the expected Kullback-Leibler distance between the posterior and the prior distributions' -- Eq. (3): int int log[p(theta|y,eta)/p(theta)] p(y,theta|eta) d theta dy. The 'expected gain in Shannon information = expected KL(posterior||prior)' is definitionally EIG = E[H(theta) - H(theta|y,d)] = mutual information I(theta;y|d). Eq. (4) U1(eta)=int int log{p(theta|y,eta)} p(y,theta|eta) d theta dy is named 'the expected Shannon information of the posterior distribution', used 'when the experiment is conducted for inference on the vector theta'. Design 'd' (manuscript) = design 'eta' (paper); target theta and outcome y identical.", "(c) Explicit info-utility <-> criterion link. p.275: 'a utility function based on Shannon information leads to Bayesian D-optimality in the normal linear model (see Bernardo, 1979).' Traces to Lindley (1956) 'suggestion' (p.277) -- i.e. this paper documents the classical origin of the EIG design criterion the manuscript invokes.", "(d) Precision note (does not change verdict): chaloner's GENERAL normative criterion is expected-UTILITY maximization (Eqs 1-2), and EIG/Shannon-information gain is its canonical information-based instance (Eqs 3-4); the paper also develops non-information utilities (A/c-optimality from squared-error loss) and criteria that are NOT utility-based (Bayes E-, G-optimality, p.278). The manuscript scopes its citation to 'the information objective ... maximizing EIG', which is squarely the Eq.(3)/(4) content, so the attribution is correct. The co-citation rainforth2024modern (separately verified ACCURATE, its C038) carries the explicit modern 'EIG' vocabulary and estimation machinery."]

claim_text: BED provides a normative EIG criterion (with chaloner).
evidence: ["(a) rainforth IS a modern BED review providing the normative EIG criterion -- CONFIRMED. Abstract (lines 9-11): 'Bayesian experimental design (BED) provides a powerful and general framework for optimizing the design of experiments.' Intro (lines 48-53): 'we want to choose designs that maximize the amount of information gathered from our experiments. This desire can be formalized using Bayesian experimental design (BED) ... which provides a powerful model-based framework for choosing designs ‘optimally’ using information-theoretic principles.' Section 2.1 defines the objective and states (lines 109-113): 'As y is unknown, we cannot optimize this directly and instead target the design that maximizes the expected information gain (EIG) ... in theta'. Section 2.1 (lines 147-149): 'Our focus ... will be on maximizing the EIG defined in (2), as this is the most commonly used, and typically best-performing, approach.' => the manuscript's 'normative criterion ... select d* maximizing EIG' is exactly the paper's central objective.", "(b) The manuscript's formula matches rainforth's definition EXACTLY. Manuscript Eq: EIG(d) = E[H(theta) - H(theta|y,d)]. rainforth Eq. (1): InfoGain_theta(xi,y) := H[p(theta)] - H[p(theta|y,xi)]; Eq. (2): EIG_theta(xi) := E_p(y|xi)[InfoGain_theta(xi,y)] (Section 2.1, lines 104-113). Design 'd' in the manuscript = design 'xi' in rainforth; outcome y and target theta identical. rainforth adds (line 120) that this EIG is 'the mutual information between y and theta given xi ... or the expected Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence from the posterior to the prior' -- consistent with the manuscript's 'information objective'.", "(c) Corroboration from the manuscript's next sentence (L79): 'EIG can be approximated via variational methods~\\citep{foster2019variational}, amortized policies~\\citep{foster2021dad}, and RL~\\citep{blau2022rl}.' claim_text: EIG can be approximated via variational methods.
evidence: Direct support. Title: 'Variational Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design'. Abstract (p.1): 'its applicability is hampered by the difficulty of obtaining accurate estimates of the expected information gain (EIG) of an experiment. To address this, we introduce several classes of fast EIG estimators by building on ideas from amortized variational inference.' Introduction (Sec 1, p.2): 'we propose a variational BOED approach that sidesteps the double intractability of the EIG in a principled manner... we introduce four efficient and widely applicable variational estimators for the EIG.' Section 3 ('Variational Estimators', p.3-5) formalizes the four estimators as variational bounds on EIG(d) (Eqs 6, 9, 11, 12; Table 1). The manuscript claim at L85 ('EIG can be approximated via variational methods') is a faithful, understated paraphrase of the paper's central result.
fix: None

claim_text: EIG can be approximated via amortized policies.
evidence: Manuscript L85 (CHAR): 'EIG can be approximated via variational methods~\citep{foster2019variational}, amortized policies~\citep{foster2021dad}, and RL~\citep{blau2022rl}.' The 'amortized policies' item maps one-to-one onto DAD. Paper title: 'Deep Adaptive Design: Amortizing Sequential Bayesian Experimental Design'. Abstract (p.1): 'a method for amortizing the cost of adaptive Bayesian experimental design... DAD addresses this restriction by learning an amortized design network upfront... This network represents a design policy which takes as input the data from previous steps, and outputs the next design using a single forward pass.' Sec. 3-4: 'we introduce the concept of a design function, or policy, pi'; 'DAD looks to approximate pi* explicitly using a neural network... the design network pi_phi'; 'it learns a design policy... and we optimize the parameters of this policy rather than an individual design.' The policy is trained by maximizing the sequential PCE (sPCE) lower bound L_T(pi,L) on the total EIG I_T(pi) (Sec. 4.1, Thm 2). So 'EIG can be approximated via amortized policies [DAD]' is a faithful, understated paraphrase of DAD's central result. CONFIRMED.
fix: None

claim_text: In LLM systems the 'posterior' is implicit in model weights.
evidence: Manuscript L219 (BG, passing reference): '...conditions absent from the LLM-based systems here, where the ``posterior'' is implicit in model weights~\citep{foster2021dad}.' DAD is cited to anchor the concept of an amortized design method that carries no EXPLICIT posterior. This faithfully characterizes DAD, which is posterior-free by construction: Sec. 4 'once pi is learned, it can just be directly evaluated during the experiment... eliminating the need to estimate intermediate posterior distributions'; Sec. 7 Discussion 'DAD sidesteps this problem altogether by eliminating the need for directly approximating a posterior distribution'; Sec. 7 Conclusions 'eliminating the need to estimate intermediate posterior distributions or optimize over designs during the live experiment.' The information a posterior would carry is instead encoded implicitly in the network weights phi via the permutation-invariant pooled history representation R(h_t)=Sum_k E_phi1(xi_k,y_k) (Sec. 4.3, Eq. 17). The scare quotes on 'posterior' correctly signal this is an implicit/no-explicit-posterior regime, which is exactly DAD's design. As a BG conceptual anchor the cite is accurate; nuance (weights encode a history->design POLICY, not a stored posterior) noted in reverse_findings but not a defect for this BG use.

claim_text: Amortized EIG estimation, which remains unsolved for open-ended S.
evidence: Manuscript L223 (CHAR): '...a system must either maintain an explicit probabilistic surrogate (GNoME's route...) or use amortized EIG estimation~\citep{foster2021dad}, which remains unsolved for open-ended $\mathcal{S}$.' (a) 'amortized EIG estimation [DAD]' is accurate: DAD amortizes the sequential-BED/EIG-maximization problem and estimates the EIG via contrastive bounds (sPCE lower L_T, sNMC upper U_T; Sec. 4.1, Thms 2 & 4). (b) The negative framing 'remains unsolved for open-ended S' is CONSISTENT with DAD's own scope. DAD requires a FIXED, pre-specified parametric model: explicit evaluable likelihood p(y_t|theta,xi_t), fixed prior p(theta), and a known latent theta; Sec. 7 Limitations: 'it requires the likelihood model to be explicit, i.e. that we can evaluate the density p(y_t|theta,xi_t)'; '...requires the experiments to be conditionally independent given theta'; '...requires the designs themselves, xi_t, to be continuous.' In location finding even the number of sources K is 'assumed to be known' (App. D.1). DAD refines beliefs about a pre-represented theta; it never generates new hypotheses / searches an open-ended S. Thus the manuscript's claim that amortized EIG estimation is unsolved for open-ended S is a correct, well-bounded use of DAD (the negative claim is the manuscript's own framing, and DAD's scope supports rather than contradicts it). Minor altitude nuance on 'estimation' vs 'policy amortization' noted in reverse_findings; not a defect.

claim_text: EIG can be approximated via RL.
evidence: Direct support. Manuscript L85 (CHAR, Sec. after Eq. eq:eid): 'EIG can be approximated via variational methods~\citep{foster2019variational}, amortized policies~\citep{foster2021dad}, and RL~\citep{blau2022rl}.' The blau2022rl half is faithful. Paper Abstract (p.1): 'the problem of optimizing policies can be reduced to solving a Markov decision process (MDP). We solve the equivalent MDP with modern deep reinforcement learning techniques.' Intro (Sec. 1): BOED 'casts this problem as optimization of expected information gain (EIG)'; '...we propose to formulate the sequential experimental design (SED) problem as a Markov decision process (MDP)...and learn the design policy using reinforcement learning (RL). Since our RL agents exploit the Policy Gradient Theorem...there is no need for the EIG objective to be differentiable.' The RL return is tied to the EIG bound explicitly: Theorem 2 (Eq. 16) and Theorem 1 (Eq. 11): 'If gamma = 1 then the expected return satisfies: J(pi) = sPCE(pi, L, T)', where sPCE is the sequential prior contrastive estimation LOWER BOUND on the total EIG (Sec. 2.1, Eq. 6). REDQ (Chen et al. 2021) trains all policies (Sec. 4, App. C.1). So RL is used to optimize a design policy whose objective is a lower-bound approximation of the EIG -- 'EIG can be approximated via ... RL' is an accurate, understated paraphrase, exactly parallel to the sibling variational (foster2019variational) and amortized-policy (foster2021dad/DAD) cites in the same sentence.

claim_text: Robot Scientist's experiment selection is a domain-specific active-learning approximation.
evidence: Claim (L85): 'Domain-specific systems already use active-learning approximations---the Robot Scientist's experiment selection~\citep{sparkes2010robot}'. THIS paper never uses the term 'active learning' (grep: 0 hits; the sole 'active' at L542 = 'active compounds'). It describes only a hypothesis-driven CLOSED LOOP with experiment DESIGN to test generated hypotheses -- L172: 'It automatically generates hypotheses from the available background knowledge and model(s), designs physical experiments to test these hypotheses, carries out the experiments on a laboratory robotic system, and then analyses and interprets the results'; L639-640 (contrasting with lab assistants): the Robot Scientist does 'form hypotheses, decide on the experiments to test them, automatically analyse and interpret the results'. Adam's actual experiment design is a fixed two-factor / Latin-square assay design 'to improve the detection of quantitatively small differences above the background noise' (L226-228, L364-368) -- i.e. a design to TEST hypotheses, not an active-learning policy to SELECT the most informative experiment. The active-learning experiment-selection mechanism of the Robot Scientist is in King et al. 2004 Nature (this paper's ref [23]), which THIS review cites only as the origin of the concept (L164) without describing the mechanism. So attributing an 'active-learning ... experiment selection' to sparkes2010robot is a misattribution: correct fact, wrong paper.
fix: Attribute the active-learning experiment-selection policy to King et al. 2004 Nature ('Functional genomic hypothesis generation and experimentation by a robot scientist', Nature 427:247-252 = ref [23] in Sparkes). Either replace \citep{sparkes2010robot} with the King 2004 citation for this specific claim, or pair them (sparkes2010robot as a general Robot Scientist review + King 2004 for the active-learning mechanism). If keeping only sparkes2010robot, soften to 'the Robot Scientist's closed-loop experiment design' (drop 'active-learning').

claim_text: Deep-ensemble uncertainty sampling approximates EIG over a tractable GNN posterior; DFT provides well-calibrated computational V.
evidence: Interpretive positioning claim; components are grounded but 'uncertainty sampling approximates EIG' is the manuscript's gloss, not the paper's language. GROUNDED: deep ensembles exist -- Methods 'Deep ensembles and uncertainty quantification' p.499: 'we use n = 10 graph networks ... the uncertainty can be measured by the spread of the n outputs'; filtration uses 'uncertainty quantification through deep ensembles33' (p.80/82). DFT as well-calibrated computational oracle/V is accurate (DFT verifies every candidate). CAVEAT: GNoME's acquisition is NOT uncertainty-maximizing sampling -- Methods 'Model-based filtration' p.82: 'filter candidate materials based on energy ... a threshold of 50 meV atom-1 was used for active learning to improve the recall of stable crystal discovery.' i.e. it selects predicted-STABLE candidates (exploitation for discovery), using ensemble uncertainty to STABILIZE predictions (median/IQR), not to select high-information points. The paper never mentions EIG or expected information gain. The manuscript hedges heavily ('partial exception', 'approximates'), so this is interpretive-but-fair.

claim_text: Predicts 2.2 million candidate structures (380,000 stable).
evidence: Both figures are the paper's. '2.2 million' -- Abstract p.80: 'the discovery of 2.2 million structures below the current convex hull'; p.82: 'GNoME models found 2.2 million crystal structures stable with respect to the Materials Project.' '380,000 stable' = the 381,000 on the updated convex hull (p.81, above). The 2-tier reading (2.2M predicted -> ~381k stable on final hull) is reasonable and arguably the technically-correct nuance (the 2.2M compete for stability; only 381k remain on the rebuilt hull). CAVEAT ON TERMINOLOGY: the paper labels the 2.2M as 'stable with respect to previous work', not 'candidate structures'; the true raw candidate pool is >10^9 -- Overview p.81: 'more than 10^9 candidates over the course of active learning.' So 'candidate structures' relabels the paper's 'stable-wrt-prior' set, flattening the paper's actual 3 tiers (>10^9 candidates -> 2.2M stable-wrt-prior -> 381k on updated hull). Numbers correct; wording is a mild simplification. The METHOD clause ('runs DFT-in-the-loop active learning') is accurate (see C050).
fix: Optional: say 'predicting 2.2 million structures stable with respect to prior work (381,000 on the updated convex hull)' to match the paper's terminology, or note the >10^9 raw candidate pool if 'candidate' is meant literally.

claim_text: Runs DFT-in-the-loop active learning with uncertainty sampling approximating the BED ideal within the computational surrogate.
evidence: Same interpretive gloss as C052. 'uncertainty sampling approximating the BED ideal within the computational surrogate': 'within the computational surrogate' correctly identifies DFT as the oracle/V. But GNoME's actual acquisition is a stability/energy threshold to maximize recall (Methods 'Model-based filtration' p.82: 'filter candidate materials based on energy ... a threshold of 50 meV atom-1 was used for active learning to improve the recall of stable crystal discovery'), NOT uncertainty-maximizing sampling; deep ensembles (n=10) provide uncertainty quantification/stabilization only. The paper never uses 'Bayesian experimental design', 'BED', 'EIG', or 'expected information gain'. Fair as a hedged theoretical positioning of GNoME within the manuscript's BED framework.
fix: Optional softening: 'ensemble uncertainty quantification within a stability-driven acquisition, which the manuscript reads as a coarse approximation to the BED ideal.' Keep only if the BED-framing is explicitly the manuscript's interpretation, not attributed to GNoME's authors.

claim_text: Idea-generation study establishing quality baselines without unifying the full discovery loop.
evidence: Claim: 'idea-generation studies~\citep{si2024novelideas,baek2024researchagent} establish quality baselines without unifying the full discovery loop.' (a) ESTABLISHES QUALITY BASELINES: CONFIRMED. si2024 is a large-scale idea-generation human study recruiting 100+ NLP researchers (N=104 unique participants; 49 idea writers, 79 reviewers) collecting 298 blind reviews to compare LLM vs human ideas on novelty/excitement/feasibility/effectiveness/overall; it provides an expert human baseline + standardized protocol 'to serve as a foundation for future follow-up studies and methods work' (Sec 1), with the concrete quality result that LLM ideas are judged significantly more novel than human expert ideas (p<0.05; novelty means 5.64/5.81 AI vs 4.84 human, Table 7 / Fig 1). (b) WITHOUT FULL DISCOVERY LOOP: CONFIRMED. Study is explicitly evaluation-centric and scoped to idea generation + human evaluation only; ideation is framed as 'the very first step' and 'only one part of the research process' (Sec 1). Idea execution/verification is deferred to Phase II future work (Sec 10 Q2/Q4); the attempted end-to-end execution agent (Appendix Y, Table 18) is found unreliable (agent skips/modifies steps, mis-defines metrics; only 5/1 of tested ideas beat baseline) and left to future work. No unified generate->execute->verify loop is claimed.
fix: None

claim_text: Idea-generation study establishing quality baselines without unifying the full discovery loop.
evidence: (a) Establishes quality baselines via LLM+human evaluation: abstract states effectiveness 'in generating novel, clear, and valid ideas based on both human and model-based evaluation results' (L26-28); GPT-4 used as judge on 5-point Likert + pairwise (L573-581), each of problem/method/experiment scored on 'its own specific five criteria' (L478, L487-488, Table 12). Novel task with 'no baselines for direct comparison' (L545), so it sets its own quality benchmarks against ablations + prior hypothesis-gen methods (SciMON, Hypothesis Proposer; Table 3). (b) Does NOT unify a full discovery loop: 'in this paper, we leverage LLMs in the first phase of scientific research - specifically idea generation' (L171-173); explicitly contrasted vs Lu et al. 2024 (AI Scientist) which 'aim to automatically generate full research papers (including idea development, code writing, and experiment execution)' (L249-251); the third output 'experiment design' is a written design, not execution; Limitations concede 'validating these generated research ideas with experiments is essential to truly accelerate scientific research' (L1272-1274) -> empirical validation left undone. Both clauses of the claim are correct.

claim_text: Knowledge-graph-driven ideation; structuring semantic memory as relational corpus substantially improves hypothesis-generation quality; does not close the loop.
evidence: The manuscript (C058, main.tex L90) asserts SciMuse SHOWS that 'structuring semantic memory as a relational corpus can substantially improve hypothesis-generation quality.' The paper finds the OPPOSITE for generation. (1) Results, 'Properties of interesting research suggestions', scimuse.txt L172-177: 'On average, we find no significant difference in interest levels between projects generated using random concept pairs, high-impact concept pairs, or without concept pairs. The similarity in results for the sanity test (projects generated without a concept pair used in the knowledge graph) and those using concept pairs enables us to analyze which knowledge graph features most strongly influence the perceived interest of a research project.' (2) Supp., scimuse.txt L602-606: 'The interest levels are notably similar between suggestions generated with and without concepts from the knowledge graph.' (3) Fig. S1, scimuse.txt L633-636: three generation methods = '(1) no concepts provided by the knowledge graph, (2) random concepts... (3) predicted high-impact concept pairs' -- and their interest distributions are near-identical. WHERE the KG actually helps = SELECTION/PREDICTION, not generation: (4) scimuse.txt L224-225: 'intelligent concept pair selection alone can significantly influence interest rankings in the graph-based approach.' (5) Supervised NN uses KG features ONLY, not the generated text: scimuse.txt L200-203 'we trained a neural network on researchers' responses, using knowledge graph properties and human-evaluation rankings, without incorporating the GPT-generated text' and L278-280 'predict interest values based on knowledge graph data alone -- without needing the full suggestion text.' Also note the evaluation measured self-reported INTEREST (1-5), not 'quality'/novelty/correctness (scimuse.txt L153-155). 'without closing the loop' is CORRECT: SciMuse only generates + predicts interest; full automation is explicitly future work (Discussion L316-323: 'the main idea of the experiment has been provided by human scientists. In the future, one might envision the entire scientific process becoming fully automated -- from the generation of an interesting idea, as we demonstrate here, to its automated execution').
fix: SciMuse does NOT show that KG-structured memory improves hypothesis-GENERATION quality -- it explicitly reports no significant difference in evaluator interest between KG-based and KG-free (no-concept-pair) generation. Its KG contribution is to PREDICT/SELECT higher-interest ideas (supervised NN on KG features alone; concept-pair selection influences interest rankings). Reword to attribute the improvement to idea SELECTION / interest-prediction rather than generation, and hedge 'substantially improve ... quality' (the metric is self-reported interest, not idea quality/novelty). Also weigh the co-cited lee2025spacer: per its digest it (a) uses a keyword-impact co-occurrence graph, NOT a semantic 'knowledge graph'/'relational corpus' in the entity-relation sense, and (b) only claims generation-quality improvement via a-priori PROXY metrics (AUROC 0.737, Weaver >85% reconstruction, embedding closeness), not downstream validation -- so 'substantially improve hypothesis-generation quality' is not solidly carried by lee2025spacer either. Suggested rewrite: 'knowledge-graph-driven ideation systems (gu2024scimuse, lee2025spacer) further show that structuring semantic memory as a relational corpus can help surface and rank higher-interest research ideas, but equally without closing the loop.'

claim_text: Knowledge-graph-driven ideation (with gu2024); improves hypothesis-generation quality; does not close the loop.
evidence: CLAIM (manuscript L90): 'knowledge-graph-driven ideation systems~\citep{gu2024scimuse,lee2025spacer} further show that structuring semantic memory as a relational corpus can substantially improve hypothesis-generation quality, but equally without closing the loop.' | VERIFY FOR SPACER: (a) STRUCTURE — Sec 2.2 (Nuri): 'Nuri is a graph-based Keyword Set extraction algorithm'; Eq (2) defines edge weight w(u,v)=sum_p log2(FWCI(p)+1)/(|K(p)|-1) = 'the joint academic impact of the two keywords'; paper labels it a 'keyword graph'/'global keyword graph', NEVER 'knowledge graph'. Nuri 'does not invoke any machine learning methods or LLMs' (Sec 2.2). => a citation-impact-weighted keyword co-occurrence graph, not a semantic entity-relation KG; 'knowledge-graph / structured semantic memory / relational corpus' is a loose but not false label. (b) QUALITY IMPROVEMENT vs plain-LLM baseline — Sec 4.3 is the only such comparison: 52 theses x 7 classes (Spacer + published + GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude Opus 4, DeepSeek-R1-0528, Grok 4) embedded with Qwen3-Embedding-8B; result 'Spacer is significantly closer to top human research than SOTA LLMs, regardless of the choice of metric' (L1101) and Abstract 'outputs from Spacer are significantly more similar to leading publications compared with those from SOTA LLMs' (L29-30). BUT metric = embedding PROXIMITY / expert-alignment, an explicit PROXY: paper rejects direct a-priori quality scoring — 'being able to reliably estimate their result would mean the idea is already obvious and thus useless... any a priori evaluation of research ideas will suffer from inherent variance and bias' (L758-761). Improvement attributed to 'our architectural modifications' as a whole (L1102), with NO ablation isolating the graph; the paper's causal claim rests on 'All emergent characteristics of Spacer originate from the word selection stage' (L223). Other numbers (Nuri AUROC 0.737; >85% reconstruction pass) measure estimator predictiveness / reconstruction fidelity, NOT quality-vs-baseline. (c) LOOP — 'this materialization step is a hard requirement for any attempt at full automation' and 'In the future, we will extend Spacer to further flesh out its accepted Statement into an executable research plan' (Future Directions, L1145-1147); Grok-4 protocol is a preliminary un-executed demo (App B). => loop is OPEN; claim's 'without closing the loop' is ACCURATE for Spacer. CONCLUSION: for Spacer the citation is directionally correct (graph-based ideation system that beats plain LLMs on an expert-alignment proxy and does not close the loop), but 'knowledge-graph' over-labels a keyword-impact graph, 'substantially improve ... quality' is supported only via an embedding-proximity proxy with no KG-isolating ablation, and quality is never directly measured. Imprecise wording, not a misrepresentation -> IMPRECISE-OK.
fix: For Spacer, soften/split the bundled claim. Options: (1) reword 'knowledge-graph-driven' -> 'graph-structured' and 'substantially improve hypothesis-generation quality' -> 'produce hypotheses more closely aligned with expert/published research (embedding-space proximity vs SOTA LLMs)'; add that the gain is system-level (not an isolated knowledge-graph ablation) and that quality is proxied, not directly scored. (2) Cite Spacer specifically for graph-based ideation + open-loop, and avoid attributing a validated 'quality' gain to the KG component alone. CRITICAL: because the co-cited gu2024scimuse does NOT support the generation-quality half (selection-only), do NOT write that BOTH papers 'equally' demonstrate improved hypothesis-generation quality — the support is asymmetric; either de-bundle or qualify per-citation.

claim_text: Propose Collective Predictive Coding (CPC-MS) as a social model of science, recasting scientific activity as decentralized Bayesian inference across a community.
evidence: Manuscript L96 (CHAR): '\citet{taniguchi2024cpc} propose Collective Predictive Coding (CPC-MS) as a social model of science, recasting scientific activity as decentralized Bayesian inference across a community.' Fully supported and is the paper's central thesis. Title: 'Collective Predictive Coding as Model of Science'. Abstract: 'This paper proposes a new conceptual framework called Collective Predictive Coding as a Model of Science (CPC-MS) ... CPC-MS models science as a decentralized Bayesian inference process carried out by a community of agents. The framework describes how individual scientists' partial observations and internal representations are integrated through communication and peer review to produce shared external scientific knowledge.' Sec 1: 'Crucially, CPC-MS posits that shared external representations -- scientific knowledge in this context -- are updated through a form of peer-review process that functions as a decentralized Bayesian inference [Tan24].' The SOCIAL framing is explicit (Sec 3.1 'Social Objectivity': 'The holders of scientific knowledge ... are the scientific community as a whole rather than individual scientists', aligning with the social approach to scientific knowledge [Lon90, Kit93]). Formalized as a PGM (Fig 3, Table 1) with shared external representation w, internal z^k, observations o^k. Every term in the clause (CPC-MS; social model of science; decentralized Bayesian inference; community of agents) maps verbatim to the paper.
fix: None. Keep as-is.

claim_text: (adjacent clause attributed to CPC) peer review is a Metropolis-Hastings consensus process.
evidence: Manuscript L96 (UNCITED, adjacent clause traced to CPC): 'peer review is a Metropolis-Hastings consensus process.' The substance is ACCURATE -- the paper explicitly models peer review as the accept/reject step of the Metropolis-Hastings Naming Game (MHNG). (1) Sec 2.1: CPC rests on 'the mathematical fact that a certain type of language game ... i.e., Metropolis-Hastings naming game (Figure 2), can be acted as a decentralized Bayesian inference of a shared latent variable' [TYM+23]. (2) Fig 2 caption: 'scientific activities updating shared explicit knowledge through experiments and communications involving peer-review process. The total systemic dynamics is structurally analogous to the MHNG.' (3) Sec 2.2/2.4 step 4 'Judgment of Scientific Representations': a submitted paper w is accepted/rejected by reviewers, and (Sec 2.4) 'This corresponds to the Listener's acceptance judgment in MHNG, and when done properly, the distribution of accepted papers can be viewed as samples from q(w | {o^k_d}_k).' (4) Sec 4.1 gives an explicit MH acceptance ratio for paper acceptance between agents: 'in MHNG, w^k_d is accepted with probability min(1, P(z^k_d|theta^k, w^l_d) / P(z^k_d|theta^k, w^k_d))'. (5) Sec 3.1 invokes MCMC convergence conditions on the peer-review chain (ergodicity, i.i.d. sampling, reducibility/'blind spot' theories, periodicity). The ONLY defect is a MISSING citation: the manuscript states this without \citep, though it clearly traces to CPC. Minor precision nuance: the paper frames it as a structural analogy/model, so 'is a Metropolis-Hastings consensus process' is marginally strong vs. 'is modeled as'.
fix: Add \citep{taniguchi2024cpc} to the clause. Optionally soften the copula to reflect the paper's analogy framing, e.g. 'peer review is modeled as a Metropolis-Hastings acceptance (consensus) process~\citep{taniguchi2024cpc}' -- since the paper presents peer review as structurally analogous to the MHNG accept/reject step, not as reviewers literally computing an MH ratio.

claim_text: A collective validator (extension to multi-agent collective with shared memory w).
evidence: Manuscript L249 (Discussion, BG): 'a collective validator (extension to multi-agent collective with shared memory w)~\citep{taniguchi2024cpc}.' As a background pointer this is accurate. 'Shared memory w' maps exactly to CPC-MS's shared external / global scientific representation w: Sec 2.2 'we call w global scientific representations ... w represents a paper or a theoretical argument'; Table 2 (Nomenclature) 'w_d = External representation for the d-th target'; Fig 3 shows w as the shared parent node inferred across agents. 'Multi-agent collective' = the community of K agents performing decentralized Bayesian inference (Fig 3, Sec 2.2). 'Collective validator' = the collective peer-review/replication process by which the community accepts/rejects w: Sec 3.1 'individual biases ... get corrected in critical dialogues including peer reviews and replications', and 'such mutual criticisms can be seen as an essential part of the decentralized Bayesian inference ... which realizes the inference to the posterior distribution at the scientific community as a whole.' Caveat (does not undermine the cite): the paper has no single named 'collective validator' module -- validation is DISTRIBUTED across reviewers via the MHNG accept/reject step -- so 'a collective validator' is the manuscript's own reification/label for that distributed process.
fix: None required. (Optional, LOW: note that CPC-MS realizes collective validation as distributed peer review over the shared representation w rather than a single validator module.)

claim_text: The single-agent framing's social layer is abstracted away by SERVO.
evidence: Manuscript L251 (Discussion, BG): 'the single-agent framing's social layer is abstracted away by SERVO~\citep{taniguchi2024cpc}.' Accurate as a pointer to the source of the 'social layer' idea. taniguchi2024cpc is precisely about the social/collective layer of science that a single-agent framing omits: it extends individual predictive coding / FEP ('basically, the idea of PC and FEP are about individual cognitive processes', Sec 1) to a COLLECTIVE/social process ('the CPC hypothesis was proposed for symbol emergence ... a generative model of symbol emergence in human society'), and Sec 3 is devoted to social dynamics (Sec 3.1 Social Objectivity: knowledge held by 'the scientific community as a whole rather than individual scientists'; consensus formation through communication/peer review among many agents). Citing it as the reference for the social layer that SERVO (single-agent) abstracts away is a faithful background attribution.

claim_text: Listed under mathematics, formal.
evidence: FunSearch operates squarely in the math/formal-oracle domain described: it targets 'problems admitting an efficient evaluate function, which measures the quality of a candidate solution' (Intro), demonstrated on the cap set problem (extremal combinatorics) and online bin packing (combinatorial optimization). Inclusion in a math-formal domain list is consistent with the paper's self-description as making 'mathematical discoveries.' No mismatch.

claim_text: FunSearch validates against a formal/numeric oracle -- an evaluator function returning deterministic pass/fail.
evidence: The FunSearch half is directly supported. (1) 'The input to FunSearch is a specification of the problem in the form of an evaluate function, which scores candidate solutions' (Specification section, Nature p469). (2) The evaluate oracle is deterministic and code-executed: cap set evaluate 'Returns size of candidate_set if it is a cap set, None otherwise' via utils_capset.is_capset(); bin packing evaluate 'Returns the negative of the number of bins required... if utils_packing.is_valid_packing(bins, problem)... else None' (Fig 2a,b). (3) Deterministic pass/fail: 'Programs that were incorrect (that did not execute within the imposed time and memory limits, or produced invalid outputs) are discarded' (Evaluation section, Nature p470). The evaluator 'guards against confabulations and incorrect ideas' (Intro). So 'evaluator function ... returning a deterministic pass/fail' is an accurate characterization of FunSearch. The 'numeric oracle' phrasing is also apt: valid programs receive a real-valued score aggregated across inputs (e.g., mean).

claim_text: AI Feynman fits equations to data with deterministic search; no memory transfer across problems.
evidence: Manuscript L440 (Appendix, Physics paragraph): 'AI Feynman~\citep{udrescu2020afeynman} and symbolic-model discovery~\citep{cranmer2020symbolic} fit equations to data with deterministic search. Validation is empirical and of medium reliability, but the systems carry no memory transfer across problems: each task starts fresh.' AI Feynman half fully supported. (1) 'fit equations to data with deterministic search': the task is symbolic regression over a data table {x1..xn,y} for unknown f (paper Sec I); the core symbolic search is the brute-force module which 'simply tries all possible symbolic expressions within some class, in order of increasing complexity' -- deterministic exhaustive enumeration of reverse-Polish symbol strings (Sec II.D, Table I); dimensional analysis (null-space of the units matrix, Eq.1) and polynomial fit (solving linear systems for coefficients, degrees 0..4, Sec II.C) are also deterministic. The neural network (Sec II.E) is only an interpolator used to TEST for translational/scaling symmetry (Alg.1) and additive/multiplicative separability (Alg.2), enabling variable elimination -- it is not the search. The paper repeatedly contrasts its deterministic progress ('every time [variable reduction] occurs, it is virtually guaranteed to be a step in the right direction') against the stochastic genetic algorithm Eureqa which 'make[s] progress... by finding successively better approximations... risking getting stuck in local optima' (Sec III.B). (2) 'Validation is empirical': modules declare success when r.m.s. fit error drops below tolerances (brute force 1e-5, polyfit 1e-4; Table II), i.e. empirical fit to data, with a final symbolic-identity answer-check (simplify(f'-f)='0' in Mathematica/sympy; Sec III.A). (3) 'no memory transfer across problems: each task starts fresh': for each mystery a fresh NN is trained on 100,000 freshly generated data points (Sec II.E.1), and every simplified sub-problem is 'iteratively passed into a fresh instantiation of our full AI Feynman symbolic regression algorithm' (Sec II.E.2, II.E.3). No learned weights or patterns transfer between distinct mysteries. Faithful characterization.
fix: None needed. (Optional, non-blocking) 'medium reliability' is the manuscript's own domain coding rather than a stated figure in the paper; it is a reasonable paraphrase given the paper flags 'de facto noise' from imperfect NN fitting (~1e-3 to 1e-5) and noise-robustness limits, so no change required.

claim_text: Listed under biology.
evidence: Domain attribution is correct. BioPlanner is squarely a biology paper: title 'BioPlanner: Automatic Evaluation of LLMs on Protocol Planning in Biology' (l.3); abstract 'we introduce BioProt: a dataset of biology protocols with corresponding pseudocode representations' (l.43-45); Sec 3 'a collection of publicly available protocols that are used to evaluate the performance of LLMs on protocol generation on a large range of topics in biology' (l.243-245); filtering keeps only protocols 'related to biology' (l.262, l.1374). Grouping it in a biology-domain background list is fully supported.

claim_text: BioPlanner's V metrics are insufficient to close the loop autonomously; physical execution never reached.
evidence: ACCURATE as scoped to the automatic validation (V) metrics / autonomous loop. Every metric BioPlanner defines scores pseudocode-RECONSTRUCTION fidelity against ground-truth pseudocode, not biological validity or physical execution: 'we report the percentage of the number of correct function assignments' (accuracy, l.516), function precision/recall, normalized Levenshtein distance for order (l.543-551), argument-name precision/recall + argument-value BLEU + SciBERTScore (Sec 4, l.504-638). The self-evaluation signal is explicitly weak: 'GPT-4 only performs slightly above chance in identifying the ground truth protocol, versus LLM generations, although it is unclear whether this is because the machine-generated protocols are largely correct, or because GPT-4 is unable to distinguish correct from incorrect protocols' (l.873-876). None of these metrics asks whether the protocol would physically execute / be biologically valid -- that question is left entirely outside the automatic loop, confirming 'the physical execution question is never reached' by the metrics. CAVEAT (see reverse_findings): the paper DOES contain one human-driven wet-lab execution (E. coli, l.1064-1072) as a separate proof-of-concept demo; the claim is accurate under the intended 'V-metrics/autonomous-loop' scope but would overstate if read as 'no physical execution occurs anywhere in the paper.'

claim_text: Generates experimental protocols; validation signals weak, biological-validity metrics insufficient; effectively no search policy beyond generation.
evidence: All load-bearing clauses supported. (1) 'generates experimental protocols' -- ACCURATE: 'we use an LLM to convert a natural language protocol into pseudocode, and then evaluate an LLM's ability to reconstruct the pseudocode' (l.45-49); teacher GPT-4 'define[s] a set of pseudofunctions that suffice to execute the protocol' and converts steps to pseudocode (l.402-405); the real-world agent generates two novel protocols (l.1064-1067). (2) 'validation signals are weak' -- ACCURATE: GPT-4-as-evaluator 'only performs slightly above chance' (l.873-876), and the paper cites prior work that GPT evaluators 'prefer longer and more coherent, but not necessarily more correct generations' (l.876-878); function retrieval 'results on this task appear generally poor' (l.683); Conclusion 'we ... find that there is more to be desired in terms of performance' (l.1095-1097). (3) 'biological-validity metrics are insufficient to close the loop autonomously' -- ACCURATE: no automatic metric measures biological validity (all measure pseudocode fidelity); biological correctness is established only by manual scientist review plus one manual lab run (l.1064-1072). (4) 'reliability low-to-medium' is the manuscript's own rating, consistent with the paper's hedged performance. (5) 'effectively no search policy beyond generation' -- DEFENSIBLE: BioPlanner is a generate-then-evaluate framework with no iterative experiment/hypothesis-selection search. NUANCE (reverse_findings): the real-world demo builds a Toolformer-like CoT agent that RETRIEVES protocols/pseudofunctions from BioProt before generating (l.895-903) -- a retrieval step, though not a closed-loop search policy; 'effectively no search policy beyond generation' still holds but the retrieval component is a minor counterpoint a reviewer could raise.

A replication TE is for replicating a finding in prior human subject research.' For that reason, we created three artificial variations of TEs ...') and author-bias reflection (L557-559: 'there is a risk that the simulations will reflect the biases of the authors rather than the behavior of humans in the population'). aher neither states nor exhibits the attributed circular-validity failure mode.

claim_text: AutoKernel closes the loop via reliable empirical validation and search.
evidence: Manuscript main.tex L450 (CHAR): 'AutoML-Zero, algorithm discovery, and AutoKernel~\citep{jaber2026autokernel} close the loop via reliable benchmark/empirical validation and evolutionary or LLM-guided search. Validation is high-reliability and the loop is fully closed...'. AutoKernel-specific portion is directly supported on both axes. (A) RELIABLE EMPIRICAL/BENCHMARK VALIDATION (GPU performance measurement): 'A five-stage correctness harness, covering smoke tests, shape sweeps across 10+ configurations, numerical stability under adversarial inputs, determinism verification, and edge-case coverage, ensures that every candidate kernel is validated before any speedup is recorded' (Abstract, txt L21-24); throughput is measured empirically -- '30s for performance benchmarking via Triton's do_bench' (Sec 3.3, txt L344-345) and, in evaluation, 'CUDA event timing, 200 iterations per configuration, and trimmed mean (dropping the top and bottom 10%)' (Sec 7, txt L560-561). Reliability/firewall: 'Fixed evaluation, mutable code. The benchmark is never modified by the agent. This firewall prevents gaming the evaluation, a real risk when the candidate generator also controls the scorer' (Sec 10, txt L866-867); demonstrated reliability: 'All 34 configurations pass all five verification stages with zero failures' (Sec 7.1, txt L761-762). (B) LOOP CLOSED VIA LLM-GUIDED ITERATIVE SEARCH: 'AUTOKERNEL mechanizes this loop. An LLM agent modifies a single kernel file, a fixed benchmark harness verifies correctness and measures throughput, and the result determines whether the change persists' (Sec 1, txt L68-70); Algorithm 1 formalizes the keep/revert loop, keeping a candidate iff 'pass and t' > 1.01 * tbest' else 'git reset --hard HEAD~1' (txt L313-319), running 300-400 experiments per overnight run (Abstract/Sec 3.3). This is genuinely an LLM-guided search (the 'LLM-guided' branch of the manuscript's 'evolutionary or LLM-guided' disjunction); AutoKernel is deliberately NOT evolutionary/RL/multi-agent (Sec 2.3/Sec 10 'simplicity over sophistication'), but the disjunction is satisfied by the LLM-guided branch and 'evolutionary' covers the co-cited systems. The digest's bottom-line answer to Layer-2 was YES on both empirical validation and iterative agent-driven search (results/jaber2026autokernel.digest.md, 'Method' section). The 'fully closed / high-reliability' characterization is corroborated by the authors' own controlled harness (zero failures across 34 configs).
fix: None required for the AutoKernel-specific claim. Optional (LOW, not a defect): the class-level clause 'the residual bottleneck is interpretability---the discovered artifacts work but resist human understanding' is a weaker fit for AutoKernel than for the co-cited AutoML-Zero/FunSearch-style systems, because AutoKernel emits human-readable Triton/CUDA source with a browsable git experiment history and never names interpretability as its bottleneck (its stated limitations are the LLM code-generation ceiling and single-GPU/single-kernel scope, Sec 11). If a reviewer probes the interpretability framing, lean on the evolved-program members of the group rather than AutoKernel. No change needed to the loop-closure claim itself.

claim_text: Cited as an example of an atypicality measure for 'novel to the field'.

Method (p.469, sec 'Comparing the observed frequency...'): 'we generated a z score for each journal pair. This normalized measure describes whether any given pair appeared novel or conventional. Z scores above zero indicate pairs that appeared more often in the observed data than expected by chance, indicating relatively common or "conventional" pairings. Z scores below zero indicate pairs that appear less often in the observed WOS than expected by chance, indicating relatively atypical or "novel" pairings.' The z-scores derive from comparing observed co-citation frequencies to 'randomized citation networks' via a 'Monte Carlo algorithm' (p.468) over '122 million potential journal pairs' from '15,613 journals' (p.468). Per paper: median z = 'median conventionality', 10th-percentile z = 'tail novelty' (p.469). The belief/posterior state pf(theta;h) over the target is derived from the filtered LLM in-context distribution pLLM(theta;h) (section 3.1, lines 240-255). Verbatim (p.13, ~lines 1188-1191): 'If a particular discovery withstands this process and consistently produces results similar to those in the main experiment, it is regarded as a verified and valuable Scientific Discovery. And it is falsified otherwise.' Supported by the paper's framing that 'falsification is the essence of both human research process and the design of an AIGS system' (abstract) and the novelty claim (p.15, ~lines 1270-1271): 'FALSIFICATIONAGENT is the first agent within AI-accelerated scientific discovery systems capable of autonomously completing the falsification process, by independently proposing scientific discovery candidates, designing and executing ablation experiments, and performing verification.' (b) CONFIRMED absent: full-text grep for information gain / expected information / mutual information / entropy / Bayesian / EIG / acquisition function / active learning / posterior returns only incidental matches (e.g., 'weight_decay', 'informative'); AIGS's selection signals are benchmark performance, magnitude of performance change, and ablation reproducibility only, so it neither computes nor maximizes EIG.

claim_text: Popper runs sequential falsification experiments with statistical error control; does not compute/maximize EIG.
evidence: ["(a) SEQUENTIAL FALSIFICATION EXPERIMENTS -- CONFIRMED. Sec 2.2: 'POPPER is an agentic framework to systematically validate a hypothesis by actively designing and executing a sequence of falsification experiments.' Sec 2.4 / Algorithm 1 (App A.1): at each round i, Design Agent proposes a falsification test for sub-hypothesis h0i, Execution Agent (ReAct, Yao et al. 2023) carries it out and reports p-value pi; loop repeats over iterations accumulating evidence.", '(b) STATISTICAL ERROR CONTROL -- CONFIRMED. Sec 2.3: primary criterion is classical Type-I error control at pre-defined level alpha in (0,1); Type-I rate = sup_{P in P0} P(y-hat=1). Sec 3 Eq.(1): p-to-e calibrator ei = kappa * pi^(kappa-1); aggregated Ei = prod_{s<=i} es; reject H0 if Ei >= 1/alpha. Theorem 4 (proof App A.2): under Assumptions 1-3, E is a valid e-value and P(y-hat=1) <= alpha under H0. Sec 4.1: nominal alpha = 0.1; Table 3 shows POPPER Type-I 0.103/0.082/0.085 (DiscoveryBench/IL2/IFNG), all at/below nominal.', "(c) NEITHER COMPUTES NOR MAXIMIZES EIG -- CONFIRMED. Full-text search for 'information gain', 'EIG', 'expected information', 'mutual information', 'entropy', 'acquisition function' returned 0 matches. The paper's optimization target for experiment selection is implication strength/relevance: Design Agent (System Prompt, Listing 3) is prompted to 'maximize the implication strength of the proposed falsification test'; a Relevance Checker R(h) in [0.1,1.0] (LLM-as-a-judge, Sec 3) prunes low-relevance tests; Self-Refinement (Madaan et al. 2024) optimizes novelty/implementability/logical-relevance. No Bayesian experimental design, no information-theoretic acquisition anywhere in the paper. So Popper substitutes a falsification/relevance heuristic for any EIG/BED-optimal objective -- exactly as the manuscript states."]
fix: None needed. Claim is accurate on all three prongs (sequential falsification, Type-I statistical error control, EIG absent). Optional precision (not required): 'statistical error control' in Popper is specifically Type-I error control at alpha=0.1 via e-values/e-processes; FDR/FWER are only sketched as future work (App C), not implemented.

claim_text: How genuinely new hypotheses/representations arise falls outside current BED formulations.

Direct support (p.1, opening/definition): 'Night science is where we explore the unstructured realm of possible hypotheses, of ideas not yet fully fleshed out. In day science, we falsify hypotheses and observe which are left standing; in night science, we create them.' Also (p.1): 'The workings of night science are rarely discussed, as they seem abstract and less concrete compared to the logical description of the formal scientific method.' And (p.2, night-science-as-creativity paragraph): 'Night science is a crucial part of science, and the creativity that we find in this realm is not only needed for the generation of novel hypotheses but also, for example, for the development of new methodologies.' The cite is used to ground a conceptual point (hypothesis generation is outside formal testing frameworks that presuppose an already-represented hypothesis space); the editorial supports the generation-vs-testing distinction. NOTE: Yanai does NOT itself mention BED/EIG/Bayesian experimental design or AI; the 'falls outside current BED formulations' framing is the manuscript author's inference, appropriate as an analogical/conceptual citation, not a claim attributed to Yanai as fact.
fix: None
