claim_text: Uses a behavioral foundation model as proxy E (synthetic participant data); gives no criterion for escalating to in-vivo validation; flags synthetic data as accelerator not ground truth.
evidence: All three sub-claims confirmed. (a) 'behavioral foundation model as proxy E (synthetic participant data in lieu of human subjects)': the 'Generating data' section replaces human data collection with a cognitive foundation model -- 'Once a task is specified, the cycle proceeds to the generation of synthetic behavioral data. This is achieved using recent iterations of the Centaur model, a foundation model of human cognition' (L99-100); Figure 1 labels the step 'Cognitive foundation model produces behavioral data for proposed set-up' (L58-59); 'foundation models, like Centaur, can generate de novo data that are often indistinguishable from human behavior' (L105-106); it is conditioned on subject metadata to 'simulate not just a generic agent, but a targeted participant profile, for instance, simulating the behavior of a 30-year-old individual with high scores in obsessive-compulsive traits performing a specific planning task' (L110-112). (b) 'give no criterion for escalating to in-vivo validation': the paper asserts the endpoint of in-vivo validation but supplies only a qualitative interestingness-ranking selector, not any threshold/gate -- 'The highest-scoring discoveries can then be validated in vivo with human participants, keeping the process anchored to biological cognition while dramatically scaling up and accelerating the search for theory' (L160-161); the abstract likewise says outputs are surfaced 'for subsequent validation in real human populations' (L23) and the Discussion only calls to 'develop norms that keep automated discovery tethered to empirical validation' (L213) -- no statistical gate, threshold, cost/benefit rule, or procedure specifying when/how much to escalate. (c) 'themselves flagging synthetic data as a hypothesis accelerator, not ground truth': Discussion, failure-mode #2 -- 'synthetic data generation is not guaranteed to be a faithful substitute for humans. Behavioral foundation models may rely on shortcuts, drift toward average subjects, or fail under genuine distribution shift... A pragmatic stance is to treat such models as accelerators for hypothesis generation rather than as ground truth: they should be continuously calibrated against benchmark effects, challenged with adversarial tasks...' (L185-190). Attribution 'Jagadish et al.' is correct (Akshay K. Jagadish is first author). Manuscript characterization is faithful.
fix: None needed. Claim is accurate and well-supported. Optional (LOW) precision note: the paper does offer a loose qualitative selector for escalation ('highest-scoring discoveries can then be validated in vivo', ranked by the LLM-critic's interestingness score), so 'give no criterion' is best read as 'no operational/quantitative criterion (no threshold or statistical gate)', which is what the paper indeed lacks; the manuscript wording is defensible as-is.

claim_text: ERA optimizes within a fixed computational evaluator, generating expert-level empirical software judged by a held-out quality metric without physical escalation.
evidence: All three sub-claims confirmed. (a) 'optimizes within a fixed computational evaluator': Methods 'We prompt an LLM providing a description, the evaluation metric and the relevant data. The LLM produces Python code, which is then executed and scored on a sandbox... The system uses the score... to hill climb towards a better score' (l.680-683); Discussion frames the whole method as 'a search for a program whose output maximizes a quality score' (l.585-586); Intro defines empirical software as 'designed to maximize a measurable quality score' (l.55). The evaluator is fixed per task (task-defined scoring function). (b) 'expert-level empirical software judged by a held-out quality metric': abstract 'creates expert-level scientific software whose goal is to maximize a quality metric' (l.32-34); held-out metrics used across all scientific tasks -- scRNA-seq: 'report the performance on the holdout OpenProblems datasets (n=1,747,937 total cells)' scored by overall score (l.204-205); COVID: 'Google Retrospective' average WIS of 26 vs CovidHub Ensemble 29 on rolling validation window (l.358-359); GIFT-Eval: 'evaluated the solution on the held-out test set using MASE point forecast' (l.944-945); geospatial: 'validated model performance on the held out test set of 420 randomly selected images using... mIoU' (l.9838-9840); ZAPBench: MAE on 20%-per-stimulus test split (l.9875-9877); integrals: 'correctly evaluated 17 out of 19 held-out integrals to within a fractional error of less than 3 percent' (l.9968-9969). Expert-level demonstrated: 'the first work demonstrating a system that beats human performance on a wide range of relevant and well-studied scientific problems' (l.639-640). (c) 'without physical escalation': all execution is in a code sandbox (CPU/GPU only; Supplementary Table S1 lists Sandbox Type CPU/GPU with NVIDIA Tesla T4, l.19308-19320) -- no wet-lab, robotics, or physical measurement anywhere in the paper. The paper explicitly separates 'optimizing empirical predictive models' from 'genuine scientific discovery, the latter of which requires reasoning about underlying theories, causal mechanisms, and mathematical frameworks' (l.648-653), and even its 'Towards Genuine Discovery' cases (cosmic-string radiation spectrum, in-silico zebrafish) are computational/symbolic (l.9974-9996). Manuscript characterization is faithful.
fix: None needed. Claim is accurate and well-supported. Optional (LOW): if the manuscript wants to be maximally precise, 'held-out quality metric' is exact for the headline scientific benchmarks (scRNA-seq holdout, COVID rolling validation, GIFT-Eval/ZAPBench test splits, held-out integrals); a couple of ablations (Best-of-N Table 1) and the Kaggle development benchmark are scored on a validation set / public leaderboard rather than a strict test holdout -- but these are not what the sentence points to, so no change is required.

claim_text: A-Lab couples computational screening to robotic synthesis, reportedly realizing 36 of 57 targeted compounds; escalates by a fixed screen-then-synthesize rule.
evidence: NUMBER is wrong. This PDF states 41 of 58 in every location, never 36 of 57: abstract 'the A-Lab realized 41 novel compounds from a set of 58 targets' (l.59); Results 'the A-Lab synthesized 41 of the 58 target compounds over 17 days of continuous experimentation, representing a 71% success rate' (l.248-250); Outlook 'successfully realized 41 of 58 novel inorganic crystalline solids' (l.770). '36' and '57' as a target count appear nowhere in the paper (grep confirms). The clause '36 of 57' is therefore unsupported by the cited source (INACCURATE-NUM). The rest of the sentence is supported: (b) 'couples computational screening to robotic synthesis' is accurate -- 'Given a set of air-stable target materials... screened using the Materials Project, the A-Lab generates synthesis recipes using ML models... and then performs these recipes with robotics' (l.93-96). (c) 'fixed screen-then-synthesize rule rather than an evidence-informed criterion for when physical validation is warranted' is a fair characterization of the VALIDATION-DECISION policy: the 58 targets are fixed by an upfront screening funnel (432->146->58 selected, l.1052-1061) and ALL are synthesized ('The experiments reported in this study represent the first attempts by the A-Lab to synthesize any of these targets', l.202); the active-learning ARROWS3 loop is invoked only AFTER a committed target's initial recipes fail >50% yield (l.215-219), i.e. it optimizes recipes for targets already chosen for validation, not whether to validate. So (c) does not warrant a MISCHARACTERIZATION flag; only the number is defective.
fix: TIER-2 OVERRIDE (orchestrator web-verified the 2026 Author Correction): do NOT revert to 41/58. The original A-Lab (this eScholarship vault PDF) reports 41/58, but the 2026 Author Correction (Nature 2026, doi:10.1038/s41586-025-09992-y; PMC12872444) reduced the confirmed successes to 36 (4 inconclusive from XRD, plus Zn2Cr3FeO8 removed for training-data contamination), framed as '36 of its 40 reported successes'. The manuscript's hedged 'reportedly realizing 36 of 57' reflects this corrected count (36 confirmed of 57 targets after removing the 1 contaminated target) and is the more accurate/current figure -- KEEP it. Recommended improvement (MED): cite the 2026 Author Correction alongside szymanski2023alab so the reduced figure is traceable (currently 36/57 is not verbatim in either the original report or the correction's own '36 of 40' framing). SEPARATE PROVENANCE ISSUE (not a manuscript error): the vault PDF is the uncorrected 41/58 eScholarship version and should be replaced with the corrected Nature version of record.

claim_text: ORGANA automates the physical execution of wet-lab experiments.
evidence: Claim (L234, CHAR): 'Robotic chemistry platforms such as ORGANA~\citep{darvish2024organa} automate the physical execution of wet-lab experiments...'. Directly and precisely supported. (1) Abstract (organa.txt L62-70): 'we introduce Organa, an assistive robotic system that automates diverse chemistry experiments using decision-making and perception tools. It makes decisions with chemists in the loop to control robots and lab devices... claim_text: Strong task performance need not imply an internalized world model.
evidence: The paper's central, empirically-supported thesis is exactly C124's claim. Abstract (lines 29-33): 'foundation models can excel at their training tasks yet fail to develop inductive biases towards the underlying world model when adapted to new tasks.' Physics case: a 109M-parameter transformer (line 766) predicts orbital trajectories at R^2 > 0.9999 (line 689) yet, when fine-tuned to predict force vectors, recovers a nonsensical, non-Newtonian force law that differs for every galaxy sample (Fig 1; Table 1, lines 791-833). Othello/lattice: models generate legal moves ~90-100% of the time (lines 1087-1089; Table 7) but have weak inductive bias toward the true board/state, grouping distinct states by legal-next-token equivalence (D-IBq= < D-IBq!=, Table 9). Method (Fig 2, sec 2.3): the 'inductive bias probe' repeatedly fine-tunes the foundation model on small synthetic datasets consistent with a POSTULATED world model, extracts the learned/extrapolated functions, and compares them to the world model's functions -- via R-IB (respect state, Eq 1) and D-IB (distinguish state, Eq 2) for finite-state binary cases, or extrapolative predictability vs. an oracle (Eq 3-6; 45-deg calibration line = perfect, Fig 4). Conclusion (lines 1283-1288): 'while many sequence models excel at next-token prediction tasks, they often have limited inductive bias toward genuine world models.' The manuscript's hedged phrasing 'need not imply' matches the paper's own boundary (models are not claimed to have NO world model; Lemos et al. 2023 recover Newton's law with architecturally imposed inductive biases, lines 1245-1251).

This diversity should be around long enough so that individuals do not discard theories too quickly, but also not stay around so long as to hinder the convergence to one action' (L791-794). (4) Method matches 'bandit/network models': multi-armed bandit problems + social networks (cycle/wheel/complete + exhaustive size-6 enumeration) + Bayesian beta-distribution learning; simulations show diversity lets the community avoid premature convergence on an inferior method (Sec 2-4, Figs 3-7). (5) 'epistemic communities' is Zollman's own framing: the analysis is generalized from scientists to 'any epistemic agents' and the reliability of 'the community as a whole' (Conclusion L822-824), and his companion model is titled 'The communication structure of epistemic communities' (Zollman 2007, refs L911-912). claim_text: Standard sufficient condition for Bayesian posterior consistency (well-specified identifying likelihood with divergent accumulated info on compact Theta).
evidence: LAYER 2: The book explicitly presents the standard sufficient conditions for Bayesian posterior consistency. Sec. 6.4 states (p. 138): 'Schwartz's theorem is the basic result on posterior consistency for dominated models. It has two conditions: the true density p0 should belong to the Kullback-Leibler support of the prior, and the hypothesis p = p0 should be testable against complements of neighborhoods of p0.' Formalized as Theorem 6.16 (Schwartz), p. 138: 'If p0 in KL(Pi) and for every neighborhood U of p0 there exist tests phi_n such that P0^n phi_n -> 0 and sup_{p in U^c} P^n(1 - phi_n) -> 0, then the posterior distribution ... is strongly consistent at p0.' KL property defined at Def. 6.15, p. 138. Posterior consistency = contraction of the posterior to a Dirac at the truth (Def. 6.1, p. 123; Prop. 6.2 'Consistency by convergence to Dirac', p. 124), matching the claim's 'b_t -> delta_{theta*}'. The citation is used correctly as a textbook reference for standard posterior-consistency sufficient conditions. LAYER 3: The claim's exact conditions (well-specified + identifying likelihood, divergent accumulated information, compact Theta => posterior -> Dirac at theta*) are a correct specialization of the book's Schwartz-type theory. Example 6.19 (Finite-dimensional models), p. 140-141: 'If the model is smoothly parameterized by a finite-dimensional parameter that varies over a bounded set, then consistent tests as required in Theorem 6.16 exist under mere regularity conditions.' Thus on a compact/bounded parameter set the testing condition holds automatically, and well-specification + identification (KL-separation, i.e. divergent information distinguishing theta from theta*) supplies the KL-support condition, yielding strong consistency b_t -> delta_{theta*} via Theorem 6.16. The paper's phrasing is an informal parametric gloss of the general Schwartz conditions rather than a verbatim restatement (the book's canonical conditions are 'prior KL-positivity + tests', not literally 'divergent information + compactness'), but it is a faithful and standard specialization, so the citation is used accurately.

claim_text: Under theta-dependent (non-affine) coupled validator, posterior concentrates on the KL projection theta-dagger, distinct from theta-star.
evidence: Direct support (LAYER 2). L408 App-Formal claim: 'the posterior concentrates on the Kullback-Leibler projection theta-dagger [berk1966], in general distinct from theta-star.' Berk's Main Theorem (Sec 4, p.54): 'If {X_i} are actually e.i.d. with distribution F, and Assumptions (i)-(iv) hold, then P_k is almost surely [F] asymptotically carried on a set A0 (see Equation (2.3)) in the sense that if U is an open set containing A0, lim P_k U = 1 [F].' The carrier is A0={theta: eta(theta)=eta*} (Eq.2.3, p.53), the MAXIMIZERS of eta(theta)=E_F log f(X|theta). Eq.(2.2, p.53): 'eta(theta) <= E log f(X); there is equality in (2.2) precisely when f(x|theta) is also a density for F. In a sense then eta(theta) measures the similarity between F and f(.|theta)' -- Berk labels eta 'essentially a measure of entropy; see [6]' (Kullback, Information Theory and Statistics). Maximizing E_F log f(X|theta) is identically minimizing KL(F||f_theta) (the E_F log f_F term is theta-free), so A0 = the KL-projection set = theta-dagger. 'Distinct from theta-star' is directly supported by Sec 5 (p.56-57): the correct-model case where f(.|theta_0) is a density for F gives A0={theta_0} only as a SPECIAL case; when the model is incorrect A0 is a different point/set (e.g. a misspecified normal model concentrates on the true mean/covariance EX, (EX,Cov X), NOT on any model 'true value'). The manuscript's 'theta-dependent (non-affine) coupled validator' inducing misspecification is a valid instance of Berk's F-not-in-model setup. Citation is accurate and correctly attributed.
fix: None

claim_text: A coupled misspecified update likelihood places the posterior at its KL minimizer.
evidence: Direct support (LAYER 2). L411 App-Formal claim: 'A coupled, misspecified update likelihood places the posterior at its KL minimizer [berk1966].' Berk explicitly works with a REWEIGHTED/coupled update likelihood: Sec 2, Eq.(2.1, p.52): 'This equation [posterior 1.1] remains valid if f(.|theta) is replaced by a function of the form u(x|theta)=g(x)f(x|theta)', following Le Cam's device u=f(.|theta)/f(.|theta_0). Setting H(.|theta)=log u(.|theta), the entropy functional is eta(theta)=E_F H(X|theta) and the asymptotic carrier A0={theta: eta(theta)=eta*} is its argmax (Eq.2.3). Main Theorem (Sec 4, p.54) then places the posterior on A0: lim P_k U = 1 [F] for open U superset A0. Since A0 maximizes E_F log (model-likelihood), it is the KL-minimizer of the (coupled, possibly misspecified) update likelihood toward the true F (Eq.2.2 equality iff the likelihood is a density for F). Thus 'a coupled, misspecified update likelihood places the posterior at its KL minimizer' is exactly Berk's Main Theorem specialized to a single (unique) minimizer. Accurate and well-attributed; the coupled/reweighted-likelihood framing matches Berk's u(x|theta)=g(x)f(x|theta) construction unusually closely.

loc: p.289 (support points vs prior dispersion); p.279 (linear large-n / diffuse-prior collapse); p.287 (local optimality as crude approx)
claim_text: [reverse] A signature qualitative result of BED per chaloner1995: for NONLINEAR models the number of support points in an optimal Bayesian design increases as the prior distribution becomes more dispersed (Chaloner & Larntz 1986, 1989), enabling model checking; local (point-prior) optimality is a 'crude approximation' to expected utility. For LINEAR models with sigma^2 known, Bayesian and non-Bayesian designs coincide as n grows or the prior becomes diffuse -- 'when a noninformative prior distribution is used ... there is no advantage to using the Bayesian approach for design' (p.279). | mismatch: None with C036/C037. Advisory context for other manuscript claims about approximate/uncertainty-based acquisition: chaloner frames local optimality and normal/Fisher-information approximations as APPROXIMATIONS to the exact expected-utility (EIG) ideal, which supports characterizing heuristic uncertainty-sampling schemes as 'approximating' BED but not as exact EIG maximization.
